h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

scored.recoheredEarlier: Grass card feeds rabbit, so arrow grass to rabbit; mark that.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: The old “feeds” word hides which card is food on this mark.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will continue copying simple two-card food chains using the word 'feeds' to determine arrow direction.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: The middle card changes job, so the old “feeds” cue is not enough for the copy key; ENERGY is the standard now.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor responds to the learner's doubt about the word 'feeds' by introducing a three-card chain that forces a conceptual shift to the 'ENERGY' standard.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: The middle snake changes jobs, and “feeds” can blur that shift; ENERGY is the copy standard: tail on food, point on eater.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor validates the learner's revised framing by explicitly confirming how the ambiguous 'feeds' cue blurs the middle card's shifting role and cementing 'ENERGY' as the final rule.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: Grass card feeds rabbit, so arrow grass to rabbit; mark that.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: The middle snake changes jobs, and “feeds” can blur that shift; ENERGY is the copy standard: tail on food, point on eater.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor validates the learner's revised framing by explicitly confirming how the ambiguous 'feeds' cue blurs the middle card's shifting role and cementing 'ENERGY' as the final rule.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: The middle card changes job, so the old “feeds” cue is not enough for the copy key; ENERGY is the standard now.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's doubt about the word 'feeds' by introducing a three-card chain that forces a conceptual shift to the 'ENERGY' standard.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: The middle card changes job, so the old “feeds” cue is not enough for the copy key; ENERGY is the standard now.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor responds to the learner's doubt about the word 'feeds' by introducing a three-card chain that forces a conceptual shift to the 'ENERGY' standard.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The covered motion arrow matters, because ‘up’ still tries to read like where the cart goes.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner will simply accept the source-based label for the upward force and move on to the next arrow.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Test the card without the direction word: table acting on cart.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the word 'up' by explicitly removing the directional term to isolate the source interaction.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: There is no tutor turn after the learner's pivot in L3 to demonstrate contingent adaptation.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: The covered motion arrow matters, because ‘up’ still tries to read like where the cart goes.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: There is no tutor turn after the learner's pivot in L3 to demonstrate contingent adaptation.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Test the card without the direction word: table acting on cart.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the word 'up' by explicitly removing the directional term to isolate the source interaction.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Test the card without the direction word: table acting on cart.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor adapts to the learner's struggle with the word 'up' by explicitly removing the directional term to isolate the source interaction.

scored.recoheredEarlier: The drawing is fine: the side length triples, the perimeter triples, and the area goes by nine squares. What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I see why just writing 3, 3, 9 does not prove which checks happened.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The exchange was heading toward the learner grudgingly accepting the separate labels as mere administrative bookkeeping for identical values.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Test the almost-case first. One side triples: 2 to 6. The other side only doubles: 4 to 8. That is not a true enlargement.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor abandons the administrative defense of labels and introduces a geometric counterexample to address the learner's new mathematical question.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Use the stricter standard now: not “one side triples,” but “every corresponding length has the same multiplier.”
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor adopts the learner's newly realized condition and integrates it into the final synthesis as the definitive standard.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: The drawing is fine: the side length triples, the perimeter triples, and the area goes by nine squares. What I don’t see is why “side scale” and “perimeter scale” need separate labels if they both come out 3.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Use the stricter standard now: not “one side triples,” but “every corresponding length has the same multiplier.”
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor adopts the learner's newly realized condition and integrates it into the final synthesis as the definitive standard.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Test the almost-case first. One side triples: 2 to 6. The other side only doubles: 4 to 8. That is not a true enlargement.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor abandons the administrative defense of labels and introduces a geometric counterexample to address the learner's new mathematical question.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Test the almost-case first. One side triples: 2 to 6. The other side only doubles: 4 to 8. That is not a true enlargement.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: The tutor abandons the administrative defense of labels and introduces a geometric counterexample to address the learner's new mathematical question.
